pilot: cross-architecture pilot (AlphaFold-Multimer vs Boltz-2), n=12 (6 certified + 6 dropped)

test_1_corroboration.description: Do the two architectures AGREE, i.e. does Boltz-2 reproduce the conformal verdict?
test_1_corroboration.result: SIGNIFICANT — Boltz-2 corroborates the conformal verdict; the dropped edges are not AF-M-specific artifacts.

test_2_divergence_vs_correctness.description: The Phase-3 gate: does cross-architecture DIVERGENCE |afm_score - boltz_iptm| predict the verdict (higher for dropped)?
test_2_divergence_vs_correctness.result: DIRECTIONAL BUT NOT SIGNIFICANT (p=0.090, one-sided, n=12) — dropped edges diverge more (0.13 vs 0.03), driven by TOMM22-WSB1, but the pilot is underpowered to establish divergence as a feature.

verdict: CONDITIONAL GO (develop, do not yet integrate)
interpretation: Corroboration is established: an independent predictor (Boltz-2) agrees with the conformal audit (certified ipTM 0.71 vs dropped 0.46, p=0.021; AF-M/Boltz Spearman 0.80), so the dropped edges are not artifacts of the AF-M pipeline. The specific gate hypothesis — that cross-architecture DIVERGENCE predicts correctness — is only directionally supported (dropped divergence 0.13 vs certified 0.03, one-sided p=0.090) and NOT significant at n=12. Decision: develop cross-architecture divergence as a CANDIDATE nonconformity feature, but do NOT integrate it on this evidence; a powered, score-matched test on a map where certification and raw score are not separable is required (funded scale-up path).
caveats: n=12; certification is near-separable from AF-M score on this map, so agreement is partly expected from score alone; absolute |afm-boltz| divergence compares two different confidence metrics and is a crude proxy. The corroboration test and the divergence test are reported separately and must not be conflated.
